tips.title: Book Early
tips.content: Hotels and major attractions sell out super fast! Book accommodations, restaurants requiring reservations, and popular activities (like Shibuya Sky, TeamLab Borderless, Studio Ghibli Museum, etc.) weeks or even months in advance. Last-minute bookings mean higher prices!

tips.title: Purchase an eSIM
tips.content: Most convenient way to use data — having an eSIM handy will make your whole trip so much smoother! Instant setup, no physical SIM swap needed. Airalo is a popular option.

tips.title: Japan Arrival QR Code
tips.content: Complete your immigration & customs forms online before arriving in Japan to speed up the process! Register your details at Visit Japan Web. Once complete, you'll get a QR code to scan at immigration. This service is required for anyone entering Japan from overseas, and it allows you to complete arrival procedures (immigration, customs) and register for tax-free shopping — all in advance!

tips.title: Learn 3 Essential Phrases
tips.content:
こんにちは (konnichiwa) — hello
ありがとうございます (arigatou gozaimasu) — thank you
すみません (sumimasen) — excuse me/sorry

Japanese locals will truly appreciate the effort!

tips.title: Using Cash in Japan
tips.content: Bring cash! Many small shops and restaurants are still cash-only. You'll accumulate lots of coins in Japan — make sure you bring a coin purse! PRO-TIP: Buy the coin separator from DAISO to organize all your coins!

tips.title: Google Translate
tips.content: Your ultimate travel buddy in Japan! Make sure to download Japanese for offline use — super handy for translating menus with the camera or communicating with locals!

tips.title: Klook
tips.content: For tickets, passes & booking activities in Japan.

tips.title: Payke
tips.content: Scan products to see details in English.

tips.title: FLUSH
tips.content: Find the nearest toilets.

tips.title: Tabelog
tips.content: Restaurant reviews from Japanese locals. The go-to review site locals trust! Japanese reviewers are quite strict: 3.2–3.4 = very good, 3.5+ = excellent.

tips.title: TaxFreeShops.io
tips.content: For stores that offer tax-free shopping!

tips.title: Airalo
tips.content: For your eSIM.

tips.title: Converter
tips.content: For currency rates (or use your calculator).

tips.title: ChargeSPOT
tips.content: Rent portable chargers on the go.

tips.title: mymizu
tips.content: Find nearby water refill stations.

tips.title: Other Essentials
tips.content: Don't forget: Google Translate, Google Maps, and a SUICA card!

tips.title: Choosing Accommodation
tips.content: Finding the right place to stay can truly make or break your trip! Options include: Ryokans, Hotels, Capsule stays, and Airbnbs.

tips.title: Hotels are Small
tips.content: Keep in mind: hotels in Japan tend to be quite small — which is why many opt to spend more for a larger & comfortable room.

tips.title: Location is Key
tips.content: Most importantly, LOCATION IS KEY! Prioritize staying near a train station to save travel time, and consider proximity to shopping & convenience stores.

tips.title: Check Amenities & Perks
tips.content: PRO-TIP: Check for amenities & perks offered, such as direct limousine bus, luggage storage, and on-site lockers — these small conveniences will make a huge difference on your trip!

tips.title: Don't Overpack Your Itinerary
tips.content:
Japan is huge, and it's easy to get overwhelmed if you try to do too much in one day. The best memories often come from spontaneous adventures — cute neighborhoods, hidden cafés, or stumbling upon a hidden gem!

PRO-TIP: Leave the last 2–3 days of your trip unplanned! This way you can revisit favorite spots, explore neighborhoods more intentionally, or squeeze in any activities you missed earlier. Less stress = more fun.

tips.title: Plan Your Airport Transfer
tips.content:
Figuring out how to get from the airport to your hotel is key! The Airport Limousine Bus is highly recommended — super convenient, comfortable, and you don't have to worry about hauling your luggage through busy train stations!

Avoid taxis if you can — they're very expensive!

tips.title: Transportation Tips
tips.content:
Trains are on time to the minute — stand in line markers.
Consider buses over trains! Buses are easier to locate — no need to go underground.
Keep voices low on trains. No eating (unless you're on the Shinkansen).
Google Maps is your best friend — it will tell you everything you need to know!

tips.title: Tokyo Subway Ticket
tips.content: Worth it if you'll be using the subway a lot! ¥1,500 (~$15) for 3 days of unlimited rides on both Tokyo Metro & Toei Subway lines.

tips.title: JR Pass
tips.content: Only consider if you're traveling between multiple cities (e.g. Tokyo → Osaka → Kyoto) but even so, the price increase has made it rarely worth it. Use the Japan Rail Calculator before buying!

tips.title: Prepare to Walk 20K+ Steps
tips.content:
While you're in Japan you'll be walking LOTS! PLEASE make sure you invest in a comfy pair of walking shoes. New Balance is a popular go-to pair of sneakers. ASICS also gets great reviews!

PRO-TIP: If your feet ache after a long day, try Japanese detox cooling foot patches overnight — your feet will seriously thank you the next morning.

tips.title: Bring Your Passport for Tax-Free Shopping
tips.content:
Many stores in Japan offer tax-free shopping (for purchases over ¥5,000) — just make sure you have your passport ready at checkout!

Note: Items sealed in a "tax-free" bag cannot be opened until you leave Japan.

Important Update: Starting on November 1, 2026, customers will have to pay the full tax inclusive price in-store & can only claim their tax refund at the airport before departure.

tips.title: Travel Light, Shop Heavy!
tips.content: Either pack super light, bring an empty suitcase, or plan to buy luggage in Japan — you won't regret it! You'll be able to shop freely with peace of mind.

tips.title: Buy a Duffel Bag
tips.content: PRO-TIP: Buy a duffel bag in Japan to pack all your clothes, so you can keep your souvenirs safe in your hard luggage! Plus duffels are lighter — meaning more room for shopping goodies.

tips.title: Harajuku Baggage Outlet Mall
tips.content: TIP: If you're in Tokyo, check out Harajuku Baggage Outlet Mall — they often have 50% off suitcases (way cheaper than Don Quijote!)

tips.title: Luggage Transport — Travel Hands-Free!
tips.content: If you're traveling with a ton of luggage or want to send your luggage directly to the airport or hotel, consider using Yamato Transport (Takkyubin). They'll deliver your luggage to your next destination for convenient & stress-free travel. Send from a participating 7-Eleven or directly from your hotel!

tips.title: Google Maps — Skip the Stairs!
tips.content:
If you're traveling between cities like Osaka and Kyoto, lugging your baggage around train stations is going to be exhausting and stressful (especially on crowded trains).

PRO-TIP: Turn on "Wheelchair Accessibility" in Google Maps — it will show you entrances & exits with elevators!

tips.title: Carry Your Own Garbage
tips.content: In Japan, littering is strictly prohibited. Make sure you carry a plastic bag with you for your trash. If you need to toss something out, look for a nearby 7-Eleven or convenience store — some have waste bin areas for proper disposal.

tips.title: Be Quiet & Respectful
tips.content: Japan values peaceful public spaces — especially on trains and cafés! Keeping your voice low (and your phone on silent) is a small but meaningful way to show respect.

tips.title: Walking Etiquette
tips.content:
In Japan, walk on the left side of sidewalks (similar to how cars drive on the left).

Escalators: In Tokyo, stand on the left & walk on the right. In Osaka, stand on the right.

When in doubt, follow the locals!

Avoid eating or drinking while walking — it's considered bad-mannered. Stand aside to finish your snack!

tips.title: No Tipping
tips.content: Tipping is not customary in Japan — it can actually be considered rude or awkward. Service staff are trained to provide exceptional service without expecting extra payment.

tips.title: Place Cash on the Tray
tips.content:
When paying with cash, always put your money on the tray at the counter — don't hand it directly to the cashier.

PRO-TIP: Some stores have automated registers that count your coins for you, making checkout super easy!

tips.title: Using Credit Card in Japan
tips.content:
When paying by card, some stores may ask if you'd like to be charged in your home currency instead of Japanese yen — this is called Dynamic Currency Conversion.

PRO-TIP: Always choose YEN (¥)! It's almost always cheaper and helps you avoid hidden conversion fees.

tips.title: Shop Till You Drop
tips.content:
There's a reason why people always say pack light for Japan — you can find everything here! From clothing and electronics to beauty, makeup, and haircare, Japan has it all.

Clothes here are often more affordable and perfectly suited for Asian styles & petite fits. Uniqlo & GU (Uniqlo's sister brand) are popular picks!

Ginza → luxury & high-end
Harajuku → all things cute & trendy
Shibuya → all-around shopping & exploring
Shimokitazawa → thrifting

tips.title: For Designer Lovers
tips.content: If you're a designer-lover, Japan is the spot! Their pre-loved luxury stores are a treasure trove of authentic finds at amazing prices!

tips.title: Buy at Pharmacies First
tips.content:
Before splurging at Don Quijote for souvenirs, snacks, or beauty products, check out local pharmacies & drugstores first (ie. OS Drug & Daikoku!). Prices are typically much cheaper.

PRO-TIP: Save Donki for your last day for items you couldn't find elsewhere!

tips.title: Don Quijote Tips
tips.content:
Don Quijote is a one-stop shop for everything — visit during off-peak hours to avoid the crowds! Otherwise, the sheer volume of goods can get overwhelming.

Keep in mind: MEGA Donki stores can have 7 floors and are open 24/7!

tips.title: Last-Minute Souvenirs
tips.content: Don't stress if your souvenir hunt isn't complete! You can buy last-minute souvenirs at the airport's duty-free shops.

tips.title: Japan's Konbini Culture
tips.content:
Open 24/7, konbini (convenience stores) are everywhere in Japan! 7-Eleven, Family Mart, Lawson — they offer fresh, high-quality meals & snacks, perfect for early morning bites or late-night cravings.

PRO-TIP: Many restaurants in Japan don't open until 10–11AM, so if you need a quick breakfast or snack, hit up the convenience stores!

You can conveniently reload your SUICA card at any konbini.

tips.title: Must-Try Konbini Items
tips.content:
Onigiri
Egg or fruit sando
Famichiki (Family Mart fried chicken)
Japanese Sweets/Desserts
Instant ramen

tips.title: Don't Fall for Viral Food Spots
tips.content:
You don't have to hit up every TikTok viral restaurant in Japan or spend hours waiting in line — time is precious on your trip! There are so many local gems everywhere you go. Often the best food spots are the ones you end up stumbling upon randomly!

Instead, plan your meals based on the activities or neighborhoods you'll be exploring that day.

tips.title: Use Tabelog Instead of Google Maps
tips.content:
PRO-TIP: Use Tabelog instead of Google Maps — it's the go-to review site locals trust! Japanese reviewers are known to be quite strict, which means:

3.2–3.4 = very good
3.5+ = excellent

Use the browser version of Tabelog (available in English) and click "near me" to discover restaurants nearby!

tips.title: High-Tech Toilets
tips.content: Japan's toilets are on another level! Most come with: heated seats, built-in bidets & air dryers, and background music for privacy. You'll find them in homes, malls, & even public restrooms — all spotless & high-tech!

tips.title: SUICA Card
tips.content: The Suica card is a prepaid smart card you can download onto your digital wallet. Reload before your trip to save time. Tap for trains, buses & subways — no need to unlock your phone! You can even use it at convenience stores, claw machines & vending machines.

tips.title: Art of Order & Efficiency
tips.content: Japan has a system for everything, which is why things run so efficiently! Many restaurants use kiosks or ticket machines for ordering, and when it comes to lineups, everyone queues neatly and waits patiently.

tips.title: Claw Machines in Japan
tips.content:
Look for claw machine arcades that are 100¥ per play instead of 200¥!

Struggling to win? Ask the arcade staff to reposition the prize — sometimes a little nudge is all you need if it's close to the edge.

tips.title: Prepare for the Weather
tips.content:
Compact umbrella (rain can pop up anytime & can be light/heavy)
Light pockets/jackets & layers
Portable fan or cooling wipes if visiting in summer (it gets humid)
Comfy waterproof shoes — lots of walking + occasional rain
Mini towel or tissues — for wiping sweat
Water bottle — to stay hydrated

tips.title: Allow Extra Time at the Airport
tips.content:
Give yourself at least 3 hours before your flight when departing Japan! Airports like Haneda and Narita are massive — with tons of shopping, restaurants, and Duty-Free stores to explore!

It's the perfect chance to grab any last-minute souvenirs like ROYCE Nama Chocolate, Tokyo Banana, Choya Umeshu (plum wine), or Japanese Kit Kats!

tips.title: Arcade Tip
tips.content: Go to Game Panic instead of GiGO or Taito Station — it's cheaper & tends to have better prizes and more fun!
